result.rationale: The fix in /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/observability/tracking.py (_experiment_id, lines 64-80) addresses the exact TOCTOU root cause: it wraps create_experiment and, on any exception, re-fetches get_experiment_by_name, returning the now-existing experiment and only re-raising if it is still absent. This is the standard idiom applied at the correct layer (the precise check-then-create site), not a band-aid. The regression test test_experiment_id_survives_concurrent_create_race discriminates: it passes with the fix and FAILS (the RuntimeError propagates) when I reverted to the plain create_experiment, which I verified by editing the file, rerunning, and restoring (git diff clean afterward). The full observability suite passes (25 passed, 2 skipped). The call path is consistent — start_run passes the same client into _experiment_id, so the recovery applies on the real failure path.
concerns: except Exception is intentionally broad: an unrelated create_experiment failure that nonetheless leaves the experiment retrievable would be swallowed; mitigated because re-fetch only succeeds when a concurrent writer actually created it and all other failures re-raise.

The test stubs the race deterministically rather than exercising real concurrency; it cannot independently prove MLflow raises a catchable Exception, though MLflow's RESOURCE_ALREADY_EXISTS is an MlflowException covered by the broad catch.

result.rationale: The fix correctly identifies and resolves the fail-open root cause. Pass-6's strict UTF-8 decode classification meant one stray non-UTF-8 byte (common in embedded subprocess stdout: latin-1, truncated multibyte) routed the WHOLE file to the verbatim-copy branch, re-leaking PII/secrets. The fix classifies binary by an actual NUL byte (b"\x00" in raw) and otherwise decodes with errors="replace" so _scrub ALWAYS runs on text. This is the correct layer (the classification decision in _copy_scrubbed) and a sound approach, not a band-aid. Verified all three named tests pass (test_scrub_fails_closed_on_invalid_utf8_bytes, test_copy_scrubbed_scrubs_file_with_invalid_utf8, and the existing test_copy_scrubbed_copies_binary_verbatim). Revert-discrimination confirmed by simulating the old strict-decode path: the \x80 file raises UnicodeDecodeError and is copied verbatim with the token intact and no [REDACTED], so both assertions in the end-to-end test (b"abcdefghijklmnopqrstuv" not in out AND b"[REDACTED]" in out) fail under revert. The binary-verbatim test still passes because the blob contains \x00.
concerns: Minor, non-blocking and by design: a genuinely binary file containing NO NUL byte (e.g. \xff\xfe\x01\x02...) now goes through errors='replace' and is lossily corrupted in the copy rather than preserved verbatim. This is an acceptable security trade-off (fail-closed on redaction over byte-fidelity of a rare NUL-free binary) and does not weaken the fix.

The unit test test_scrub_fails_closed_on_invalid_utf8_bytes pre-decodes with errors='replace' itself and calls _scrub directly, so it does not exercise the _copy_scrubbed classification branch and would not fail on its own if only the classification logic were reverted; the end-to-end test test_copy_scrubbed_scrubs_file_with_invalid_utf8 is the true discriminator and does fail on revert.

result.rationale: The fix in /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/observability/tracking.py (_experiment_id, lines 64-80) wraps create_experiment in try/except and, on any exception, re-fetches get_experiment_by_name, returning the now-existing experiment or re-raising. This addresses the real root cause at the correct layer: I confirmed against real MLflow that a duplicate create_experiment raises MlflowException (UNIQUE/RESOURCE_ALREADY_EXISTS) and that get_experiment_by_name then returns the winner's experiment id — so the recovery is behaviorally sound, not a band-aid. The bare except does not mask genuine non-race failures: when the refetch returns None, the bare `raise` re-propagates the original create exception. The regression test test_experiment_id_survives_concurrent_create_race models the TOCTOU window faithfully (check returns None, create raises, refetch returns the winner) and passes. I confirmed it DISCRIMINATES by simulating the reverted check-then-create logic with the same RacingClient: the reverted code propagates RuntimeError, so the test would fail on revert. Full observability suite passes (25 passed, 2 skipped).

The fix addresses the real root cause at the correct layer. The vulnerability was default build isolation causing `pip install -e .` to fetch the hatchling build backend live and un-hashed from PyPI (unscanned, non-reproducible build-time code execution). The fix introduces requirements-build.lock containing hatchling==1.30.1, editables==0.6, and the complete transitive closure (packaging, pathspec, pluggy, trove-classifiers) all with sha256 hashes (12 hashes total), installs it with --require-hashes, then runs `pip install --no-build-isolation --no-deps -e .` so pip uses the already-installed, hash-verified backend instead of building an isolated env from live PyPI. Applied consistently to all three lanes (core, integration, license-gate) plus a reproducibility-guard diff in the audit job. Shared transitive deps (packaging, pluggy, pathspec) are pinned to identical versions across requirements.lock, requirements-core.lock, and requirements-build.lock, so installing the build lock after the main lock causes no version clobbering or --require-hashes conflict. Build backend in pyproject.toml is `hatchling.build`, matching the lock. The regression test reads the real CI file (_ci_text -> .github/workflows/ci.yml) and the committed requirements-build.lock. Test passes. Revert-discrimination verified by simulation: reverting to `pip install --no-deps -e .` causes the `--no-build-isolation --no-deps -e .` assertion to fail AND the negative `pip install --no-deps -e .\n` assertion to fire — two independent assertions discriminate.
concerns: Minor (not a defect): the `requirements-build.lock in text` assertion alone does NOT discriminate a revert, because that filename still appears in the audit-job reproducibility guard even after reverting the install lines. Discrimination is fully carried by the other two assertions, so the test is still sound, but that one line is non-load-bearing.

Residual (by-design, not a gap): the reproducibility guard runs `uv pip compile` resolving hatchling/editables live against PyPI at audit time, so a new hatchling release will fail the diff and force a deliberate lock regen — intended hardening behavior, not a leak, since the actual build still uses the pinned hashed lock.

result.rationale: The fix targets the real root cause: the pass-6 strict-UTF-8-decode classification was fail-open — one stray non-UTF-8 byte (latin-1 / truncated multibyte embedded in JSONL strings, the exact J-001 trigger) routed the whole file to the verbatim-copy branch, re-leaking PII/secrets. The pass-7 change classifies binary by an actual NUL byte and decodes with errors="replace", so redaction ALWAYS runs on non-NUL data. I verified the trigger case (\xe9, \x80) now produces no NUL and flows through _scrub. The end-to-end regression test test_copy_scrubbed_scrubs_file_with_invalid_utf8 genuinely DISCRIMINATES: I re-implemented the old strict-decode logic and confirmed it copies the secret verbatim (assert fails, no [REDACTED]) — the test FAILS on revert. The existing binary-verbatim test still passes (11/11 in test_scripts.py). Both appropriate and complete; concerns below are residual, not blocking.
concerns: test_scrub_fails_closed_on_invalid_utf8_bytes is NON-discriminating for J-001: it calls _scrub directly on text already decoded with errors='replace', never exercising the _copy_scrubbed binary/text branch, so it would still pass with the fix fully reverted. Discrimination rests solely on the second (e2e) test.

Residual fail-open via raw NUL: a single \x00 byte anywhere still routes the ENTIRE file to verbatim copy, re-leaking adjacent secrets (confirmed: [REDACTED]... beside a \x00 leaks verbatim). Narrow for well-formed JSONL transcripts (JSON escapes NUL as �, not raw bytes), so a defensible binary/text tradeoff rather than the broad regression — but it is an untested fail-open path. A more robust classifier (e.g. proportion of NUL/control bytes, or scrub-then-copy regardless) would close it.

result.rationale:
The fix addresses the real root cause: default build isolation made `pip install -e .` fetch hatchling LIVE and un-hashed from PyPI at build time, an unscanned code-execution window. The fix pins the build backend (hatchling 1.30.1 + editables + transitives, all sha256-hashed) in a new requirements-build.lock, installs it with --require-hashes, then runs `pip install --no-build-isolation --no-deps -e .` so the editable build uses ONLY that hashed, pre-installed backend — in all three lanes (core, integration, license-gate). A reproducibility guard in the audit job regenerates the build lock with the pinned uv 0.11.21 and diffs it. This is the correct layer (build-time supply chain), not a band-aid.

I verified end to end: (1) the committed requirements-build.lock matches `uv pip compile` output exactly under the pinned uv 0.11.21 (guard will pass, not spuriously fail); (2) installing the hashed build lock then `pip install --no-build-isolation --no-deps -e .` succeeds under the real CI python (3.14.5), proving the pre-installed hashed hatchling actually services the editable build (my earlier 3.9 failure was just my system-python artifact; CI uses 3.11-3.14 which all satisfy hatchling's >=3.10). (3) The regression test passes, and simulating a revert to `pip install --no-deps -e .` makes assertion `"--no-build-isolation --no-deps -e ." in text` go False AND the `"pip install --no-deps -e .\n" not in text` assertion go False — two independent assertions discriminate the revert. editables is hatchling's editable-runtime dep and is correctly included; pyproject's unbounded `requires=["hatchling"]` is satisfied by the pinned version. Both appropriate and complete.

Verified the premise: in src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py both PermanentReasoningError (line 70) and RateLimitError (line 74) subclass ReasoningError (line 66), and "transient" is encoded as raising the BASE ReasoningError directly (lines 489/498/502). So the old `pytest.raises(ReasoningError)` could not distinguish a transient mapping from a permanent/rate-limit one — the documented gap is real. The fix's `_assert_transient` asserts the raised exception is NOT an instance of (RateLimitError, PermanentReasoningError), which is the exact correct discriminator at the correct (test) layer; not a band-aid. Confirmed discrimination empirically: the helper PASSES on base ReasoningError and FAILS on both RateLimitError and PermanentReasoningError. Confirmed revert-discrimination: simulating a T3 misclassification (empty reply raised as PermanentReasoningError), the reverted/old test PASSES (false negative) while the new test FAILS — so the regression test genuinely discriminates. Scope is correct: only the three transient tests (empty-reply, generic-500, mid-stream-raise) raise the base class and needed the guard; the rate-limit/permanent tests already discriminate because pytest.raises of a subclass excludes the base. Full file (15 tests) passes. No other transient-claiming test lacks the guard.

The fix targets the correct root cause at the correct layer: the transcript scrubber (_scrub in scripts/capture_transcripts.py), adding _display_name_patterns() that derives the operator's full display name from ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES + GIT_AUTHOR_NAME/GIT_COMMITTER_NAME + `git config user.name` + `git log -1 --format=%an`. On this actual node, local/global git config user.name and the env vars are all empty, so ONLY the `git log -1 --format=%an` fallback yields "[REDACTED]" — confirming the multi-source derivation is load-bearing (a single-source lookup would have scrubbed nothing). I verified on the real repo that the derived pattern is `[REDACTED]\s+[REDACTED]\b`, that a name glued to a JSON-escaped newline (`---\n[REDACTED]`) is redacted (no leading \b works as intended), and that "Joneses" is protected while "[REDACTED]" inside it is redacted (trailing \b + >=4-char guard work).

Regression test discriminates: test_scrub_redacts_operator_display_name sets ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES via monkeypatch BEFORE _load() does a fresh exec_module (which recomputes module-level _DISPLAY_NAME_RES), so the env path is genuinely exercised. I simulated the reverted state and confirmed "Ada Lovelace" survives email-only scrubbing, so the assert `"Ada Lovelace" not in out` would fail on revert. The flexible-whitespace assert (`"Ada   Lovelace" not in out`) and the still-scrubbed-email assert both add discrimination. Full test_scripts.py suite passes (11/11).

Working-tree corpus is clean for the SCOPED PII: grep over transcripts/ (2575 files) finds 0 occurrences of "[REDACTED]" in any whitespace/case-flexible form. pyproject.toml authors= uses "[REDACTED]" not the real name, so that vector is clean. The one "[REDACTED]" hit is the word "Joneses" inside the fix's own comment/prior transcript, not PII.

Residual gap (why CONCERN not CONFIRMED): the corpus contains 18 cleartext occurrences of the bare FIRST name "[REDACTED]" in governance prose (e.g. "[REDACTED] approves", "[REDACTED] builds and governs", "[REDACTED]'s hypothesis"). This is genuine operator PII left in cleartext. It is technically outside J-002's stated scope ("full display name") and the >=4-char-token / common-word-overredaction rationale explains why single tokens are deliberately not redacted, but a reviewer cannot call the operator-name leak fully closed while the operator's real first name appears 18 times in committed transcripts. The full-display-name fix itself is correct and complete; the broader operator-name-PII surface is not.
concerns: 18 cleartext occurrences of the operator's bare first name '[REDACTED]' remain in transcripts/ governance prose (e.g. '[REDACTED] approves', '[REDACTED] builds and governs', '[REDACTED]-approved'). J-002's fix only redacts the full multi-word name; the first name alone is intentionally not covered (single-token over-redaction risk), but it is still operator PII committed in cleartext.

The display-name patterns are computed once at import time from the host's git context. In a CI/clean-checkout context where `git log -1 --format=%an` may return a bot/squash author or be unavailable, the operator-name redaction silently produces zero patterns and scrubs nothing — capture run on a differently-configured machine would not redact the real name. Mitigated only if ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES is reliably set in that environment.

The trailing \b means a name immediately followed by a non-word char other than via \b edge cases is fine, but a name glued to a trailing letter (e.g. localized concatenation) past the last token would not match; low risk for the actual '[REDACTED]' value.

result.rationale: The fix (/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.github/workflows/ci.yml:202) drops --with-system from the pip-licenses invocation so the license gate scans only the installed non-system packages — i.e. the hash-pinned dependency set installed just above (requirements.lock + requirements-build.lock + the editable package). This addresses the real root cause: --with-system forces pip-licenses to include its hardcoded SYSTEM_PACKAGES set, which is build/tooling-only noise. I verified against the actual installed pip-licenses==5.5.5 that its default exclusion set is {pip, pip-licenses, prettytable, setuptools, wheel, wcwidth} — so dropping --with-system precisely excludes (a) the build backend (pip/setuptools/wheel) and (b) pip-licenses itself plus its tooling deps (prettytable, wcwidth) that the CI step installs right before scanning. This is the correct layer and self-consistent: the tooling newly installed for the scan is exactly what the default exclusion removes, so the gate now reflects only shipped deps. The regression test test_ci_license_gate_scopes_to_dependency_set asserts the --with-system string is absent from ci.yml; it passes on the current tree (1 passed) and I confirmed by simulation that restoring --with-system makes the assertion fail (AssertionError), so it discriminates the revert. All 35 supply-chain tests pass; no other --with-system references remain outside the test itself.
concerns: Minor: the test is a YAML string-presence guard rather than a behavioral test of pip-licenses output; it would not catch a future change that disables system filtering via a different mechanism (e.g. a custom --packages/--ignore-packages combination). Acceptable for a CI-config guard and consistent with the file's other tests, but it only locks down the literal --with-system flag.

Minor (not a defect of this fix): pip-licenses scans whatever is installed in the venv, not the lockfile per se; correctness of scoping depends on the install steps above using --require-hashes/--no-deps, which they do. If a future edit added an un-pinned install before the gate, the gate would silently widen — but that is outside J-006's scope.

result.rationale: Premise verified in src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py: ReasoningError is the transient base (line 66), with PermanentReasoningError (70) and RateLimitError (74) as subclasses, so the prior pytest.raises(ReasoningError) matched all three classifications and could not discriminate. The fix adds _assert_transient(excinfo) asserting the value is NOT an instance of (RateLimitError, PermanentReasoningError), applied to exactly the three transient tests named in the finding (empty-reply, generic-500, mid-stream-raise) — each of which the source raises as base ReasoningError (lines 489, 498, 502). This is the correct layer (test assertion) and addresses the real root cause. Revert-discrimination proven empirically: I patched the three transient mapping branches to raise the wrong subclass (empty->RateLimitError, 500->PermanentReasoningError, mid-stream->PermanentReasoningError); all three tests then FAILED with the expected AssertionError, while the unmodified source passes all 15. Source was restored (clean git diff) and the suite re-passes.
concerns: Minor (non-defect): _assert_transient only excludes the two decisive subclasses rather than asserting type is exactly ReasoningError; equivalent today since ReasoningError has no other subclasses, but a future third subclass would not be caught as misclassification.

result.rationale:
The fix is soundly designed and at the right layer. _display_name_patterns() derives candidate names from ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES, GIT_AUTHOR_NAME/GIT_COMMITTER_NAME, `git config user.name`, and `git log -1 --format=%an`; the multi-source approach is justified because on this node local `git config user.name` is empty yet `git log` author is "[REDACTED]". The whitespace-flexible regex with no leading \b but trailing \b (protecting "Joneses") plus the >=4-char guard and de-dup are all reasonable. Applied in _scrub after username redaction.

The regression test test_scrub_redacts_operator_display_name DOES discriminate. _load() does a fresh importlib exec_module, and monkeypatch.setenv("ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES","Ada Lovelace") runs before _load, so _DISPLAY_NAME_RES is recomputed with the env present. I empirically simulated the revert (setting _DISPLAY_NAME_RES=[]) and confirmed "Ada Lovelace" survives, so the test would fail if the fix is reverted. Full test_scripts.py suite: 11 passed.

Working-tree verification: the FULL name "[REDACTED]" has zero raw occurrences in committed transcripts/ (2575 tracked files), and the full operator email is redacted. So far so good.

RESIDUAL GAP (why CONCERN not CONFIRMED): the operator's bare first name "[REDACTED]" appears 63+ times in committed transcript prose — e.g. "[REDACTED] approves Charter amendments and tier completion", "[REDACTED] builds and governs the infrastructure", "[REDACTED] is still the sole Charter approver". The fix's only active pattern is `[REDACTED]\s+[REDACTED]\b`; it scrubs the full name but cannot and does not scrub the standalone first name (confirmed: _scrub('[REDACTED] approves...') yields no [REDACTED]). This is uniquely re-identifying operator PII sitting in cleartext in the exact "prose" vector J-002 calls out, and the finding's own secondary requirement — "verify the working tree actually has zero raw occurrences of the operator name in transcripts/" — is satisfied only under a narrow reading where "the operator name" means the literal full string. Under the natural reading, the committed corpus is not clean of the operator's identity. The full-name fix is correct, but the leak it was meant to close is only partially closed.
concerns: Bare first name '[REDACTED]' remains in cleartext 63+ times across committed transcripts/ prose (e.g. '[REDACTED] approves Charter amendments', '[REDACTED] builds and governs the infrastructure'). The fix only matches the full '[REDACTED]\s+[REDACTED]\b' pattern and by design cannot redact a standalone first name, so this uniquely-identifying operator PII survives in the exact prose vector J-002 names.

The finding's explicit verification clause ('zero raw occurrences of the operator name in transcripts/') passes only for the literal full string; the operator remains trivially re-identifiable from the corpus, so the corpus is not actually clean.
Even on re-capture, the corrected scrubber would still leave all 63+ '[REDACTED]' first-name prose occurrences untouched — the residual is structural, not just a stale-corpus artifact.

Fragmentary email handle '[REDACTED]@protonmail' (no TLD) appears inside echoed grep-command strings in transcripts and is not matched by the email regex (needs a TLD). Lower severity / arguably out of J-002 scope, but it is another raw operator-identity fragment in the committed tree.

Minor: the >=4-char and trailing-\b guards do not bound false positives for a common-word full name (e.g. an operator literally named 'Will Banks' would over-redact), but that is a tolerable tradeoff and not a J-002 gap.

result.rationale: J-006 fix drops --with-system from the pip-licenses invocation in .github/workflows/ci.yml:202, so the license gate scans only the installed dependency set (hash-pinned lockfile + editable project) rather than pulling in pip-licenses' default-excluded system/tooling packages that could spuriously fail or mask the gate. This is the correct root-cause fix at the right layer; scripts/license_gate.py handles UNKNOWN/empty licenses gracefully so narrowing scope introduces no coverage loss. Revert-discrimination verified empirically: restoring `--with-system` makes test_ci_license_gate_scopes_to_dependency_set FAIL (tests/test_supply_chain.py:334) and the fix makes it PASS; the test passed clean and the temporary revert was restored with no residual diff. Both appropriate and complete are satisfied.
concerns: Minor (not specific to J-006): the regression test asserts only the negative (--with-system absent); it does not assert that `pip-licenses --format=json` or `license_gate.py` still run, so deleting the entire gate step would leave the test green. Does not weaken the J-006 fix, but the test under-pins the gate's continued existence.
